metadata.source: Third audit pass: plant flags and legal/caution cleanup

records.edibility_detail: Excellent edible allium, but legal and sustainability concerns matter in many places. Treat harvest pressure and local rules as part of this entry, and keep the ID warning because toxic look-alikes exist if smell is ignored.

Excellent edible allium, but legal and sustainability concerns matter in many places. Treat harvest pressure and local rules as part of this entry, and keep the ID warning because toxic look-alikes exist if smell is ignored.

Edible when correctly identified and thoroughly cooked. This entry keeps both prep and caution because only certain fiddleheads are considered safe and undercooked or misidentified fiddleheads are a real problem.
Edible when correctly identified, but the poison-hemlock / water-hemlock confusion risk is serious. This is a major caution entry, not a casual beginner root.
Edible and useful, but keep the caution strong because umbel look-alikes can be dangerous. This belongs in the conservative caution bucket.
Traditionally used as a ginger substitute, but not a carefree food plant. Keep this as tea/flavor + medicinal with caution rather than promoting it as a broad edible staple.
Edible in modest amounts, but oxalate load means this is better treated as an edible-with-caution plant than as a carefree bulk green.
Edible in modest amounts, but not a bulk green because oxalates matter for some people.
Edible and mild, but keep a light caution because oxalate notes belong here rather than pretending this is a zero-caution salad plant.
Excellent edible and medicinal plant, but this legitimately keeps both prep and caution because raw stinging hairs and handling matter until cooked, dried, or processed.
Edible for many people, but daylilies deserve caution because some people react poorly and they should not be confused with true lilies.
Edible when ripe and properly processed. Keep prep and caution because raw or poorly prepared elder material can cause stomach upset and plant-part distinctions matter.
Useful as food and tea, but keep a light caution because elder entries need plant-part clarity and should not drift into a blanket all-parts-edible assumption.
Edible when ripe in the husk, but caution belongs here because unripe Physalis material is not the same as the ripe fruit stage.
Use as tea/flavor with caution. Honeysuckle is too broad a bucket to treat as a simple edible flower entry without stronger species-level control.
Flowers are edible, but the rest of the tree is not. Keep the caution prominent so the edible-part distinction stays obvious.
Best when young and tender. This is more of a harvest-stage note than a danger flag, but prep remains fair because older stems are fibrous and usually need kitchen processing.
Historically edible with real preparation, but not a casual beginner plant. Keep both prep and caution.
Edible with preparation, but not a beginner-level casual plant. Keep both prep and caution.
Broad-use edible and medicinal plant. No strong caution flag needed here in the current framing.
Useful as flavoring and tea in small amounts, but not a bulk food and not something to treat carelessly. Keep the caution light but present.
Traditional tea plant, but keep the caution prominent because it is not a carefree daily beverage.
Useful tea and medicinal flower without needing a heavier caution flag in the current model.
Classic tea flower; this fits tea + medicinal better than broad edible tagging.
Aromatic tea and seasoning plant. Tea/flavor + medicinal is the cleaner tag set here.
Better treated as tea/flavor + medicinal than as a broad edible flower.
Better as tea/flavor + medicinal with light caution rather than as a broad edible cone entry.
A real edible/useful part and not especially a caution entry in the current framing.
Solid edible/useful cattail part; no special caution tag needed here beyond normal clean-water judgment.
Useful edible rhizome that genuinely benefits from processing, so prep belongs here.
Excellent edible, but this is one of the clearest legal/regulatory entries and should carry the legal flag explicitly.
Medicinally important and heavily regulated. This is a legal and conservation-sensitive entry, not a casual edible.
